questions.q: How many sons were born to Gandhari and Dhritarashtra?
options: Ninety-nine
One hundred and one
One hundred
One hundred and eight
questions.explanation: Gandhari bore Dhritarashtra exactly one hundred sons, the Kauravas, along with a single daughter.

questions.q: What did Gandhari deliver after her unnaturally long pregnancy?
options: Twin boys joined at the shoulder
A stillborn daughter
A single healthy firstborn son
A hard ball of flesh
questions.explanation: Instead of a child, Gandhari delivered a dense, hard mass of flesh after striking her womb in despair.

questions.q: Who divided the mass of flesh so that the Kauravas could be born?
options: The sage Vyasa
The sage Durvasa
Bhishma the grandsire
The royal physician of Hastinapura
questions.explanation: Vyasa, who had granted Gandhari her boon, arrived and divided the flesh so that her hundred sons could live.

questions.q: In what were the divided pieces of flesh nurtured until they were born?
options: Baskets of sacred kusha grass
Jars filled with ghee
Cradles of carved sandalwood
Vessels of Ganga water
questions.explanation: Each piece was placed in its own pot of clarified butter, where it grew until ready to emerge.

questions.q: Who was the firstborn of the hundred Kaurava brothers?
options: Dushasana
Vikarna
Yuyutsu
Duryodhana
questions.explanation: Duryodhana emerged first from the jars, becoming the eldest of Gandhari's hundred sons.

questions.q: How long did Gandhari carry her pregnancy before striking her womb?
options: Nine months
One year
Two years
Seven years
questions.explanation: Gandhari remained pregnant for two full years without delivering, which drove her to despair.

questions.q: Besides her hundred sons, Gandhari also bore one daughter. What was her name?
options: Sudeshna
Dushala
Uttara
Bhanumati
questions.explanation: Dushala was the only daughter among Gandhari's hundred and one children.

questions.q: Which animals howled as an evil omen at the moment of Duryodhana's birth?
options: Jackals
Elephants
Swans
Cattle
questions.explanation: Jackals howled and other beasts of ill omen cried out when Duryodhana came into the world.

questions.q: Who urged King Dhritarashtra to abandon the newborn Duryodhana?
options: Shakuni and the Gandhara envoys
Kunti and the other queen mothers
Vidura and the wise brahmins
Drona and Kripacharya
questions.explanation: Reading the dreadful omens, Vidura and the learned brahmins begged the king to cast the child away.

questions.q: Why did Dhritarashtra refuse to abandon Duryodhana despite the omens?
options: Gandhari threatened to leave the palace forever
Bhishma overruled the assembled counselors
The priests found a rite to cancel the omens
His love for his son overcame every warning
questions.explanation: Blinded by paternal affection, Dhritarashtra could not bring himself to give up his firstborn son.

questions.q: What drove Gandhari to strike her womb in despair?
options: News that Kunti had already borne Yudhishthira
A cutting taunt from her brother Shakuni
A prophecy that all her sons would perish
The pain of carrying the child past nine months
questions.explanation: Hearing that Kunti had given birth to Yudhishthira while she herself remained pregnant, Gandhari struck her womb in jealous despair.

questions.q: How had Gandhari earned the boon of a hundred sons?
options: By keeping a vow of silence for a full year
By serving the sage Vyasa with devoted hospitality
By blindfolding her eyes in loyalty to her husband
By performing one hundred fire sacrifices
questions.explanation: When Vyasa arrived weary and hungry, Gandhari attended to him so well that the pleased sage offered her a boon.

questions.q: Why was the ball of flesh divided into one hundred and one pieces rather than one hundred?
options: Vyasa feared that one piece might fail to quicken
An extra piece formed on its own inside the jar
Gandhari asked for a daughter as well as her hundred sons
The boon had secretly promised an heir for Gandhara too
questions.explanation: Gandhari longed for a daughter, so Vyasa made one extra portion, which became Dushala.

questions.q: What did Vyasa do to the mass of flesh before dividing it?
options: Wrapped it in silk blessed by the gods
Held it over sacred fire to harden it
Anointed it with soma brought from heaven
Sprinkled it with cool water, splitting it into pieces
questions.explanation: At Vyasa's instruction the ball was sprinkled with cool water, whereupon it divided into a hundred and one parts.

questions.q: Whom did Gandhari's daughter Dushala later marry?
options: Jayadratha, king of Sindhu
Shalya, king of Madra
Bhagadatta of Pragjyotisha
Susharma of Trigarta
questions.explanation: Dushala became the wife of Jayadratha, the Sindhu king who would later seal the Chakravyuha against Abhimanyu.

questions.q: Who was Yuyutsu, born during the time of Gandhari's long pregnancy?
options: Shakuni's son sent to be raised in Hastinapura
Dhritarashtra's son by a vaishya serving woman
A foundling adopted by the blind king
The hundred-and-first piece of the ball of flesh
questions.explanation: While Gandhari's delivery was delayed, a vaishya woman attending Dhritarashtra bore him the son Yuyutsu.

questions.q: Which Pandava was born on the same day as Duryodhana?
options: Yudhishthira
Arjuna
Bhima
Nakula
questions.explanation: Bhima, the Kaurava prince's lifelong rival and eventual slayer, was born on the very day Duryodhana emerged.

questions.q: What sound did the newborn Duryodhana make that deepened the evil omens?
options: A hiss like an angry serpent
No sound at all for a full day
A wail that put out the lamps
A cry like the braying of an ass
questions.explanation: Duryodhana cried out like a braying ass at birth, a sound the wise read as a terrible portent.

questions.q: What principle did Vidura cite when urging that the newborn Duryodhana be cast away?
options: Abandon one to save a family, a family to save a village
A king must love the kingdom more than his own blood
What is born of ill omens belongs to the gods alone
The eldest must yield wherever the youngest is blessed
questions.explanation: Vidura invoked the maxim that an individual may be sacrificed for the family, the family for the village, and the village for the country.

questions.q: What did the omens at Duryodhana's birth foretell, according to the wise?
options: The boy would die before his twelfth year
The boy would cause the destruction of his line
The boy would be invincible against all weapons
The boy would one day abandon the throne
questions.explanation: The brahmins and Vidura warned that this child would bring about the ruin of the entire Kuru race.

questions.q: Which Kaurava was the second-born, later infamous for dragging Draupadi by her hair?
options: Durmukha
Vikarna
Dushasana
Duhsaha
questions.explanation: Dushasana followed Duryodhana from the jars and grew into his brother's cruelest instrument.

questions.q: Counting all of his children, how many sons did Dhritarashtra have in total?
options: Ninety-nine, one having died at birth
Exactly one hundred, all borne by Gandhari
One hundred and two, counting twin heirs
One hundred and one, counting Yuyutsu
questions.explanation: Beyond Gandhari's hundred sons, Dhritarashtra fathered Yuyutsu on a vaishya woman, for a total of one hundred and one.

questions.q: How does the epic describe the mass that Gandhari delivered?
options: Hard as iron, a dense ball of flesh
Soft and glowing like molten gold
Cold and clear like a lump of crystal
Dark and coiled like a sleeping serpent
questions.explanation: The Adi Parva describes the delivered mass as a hard ball of flesh like a lump of iron.

questions.q: What instruction did Vyasa give regarding the jars that held the pieces of flesh?
options: Open every jar together at the next full moon
Guard them in a hidden place until each ripened in its time
Float them upon the Ganga for the gods to bless
Bury them beneath the palace shrine for one year
questions.explanation: Vyasa ordered the pots kept in a concealed, well-guarded spot and opened only when the embryos had matured.

questions.q: Although Duryodhana led the hundred, which prince was senior in age, a fact that shaped the succession dispute?
options: Duryodhana, born before all of the Pandavas
Bhima, born on the same day as Duryodhana
Yudhishthira, born before Duryodhana
Dushasana, eldest of all the jar-born
questions.explanation: Yudhishthira's earlier birth gave him the elder's claim to the throne, a seniority Duryodhana never accepted.

questions.q: When Dhritarashtra asked the wise whether Duryodhana could still become king, what was his underlying worry?
options: Duryodhana's jar-birth might bar him from royal rites
Gandhari's blindfold might pass blindness to her son
The evil omens might frighten away royal allies
Yudhishthira, already born, held the right of the eldest
questions.explanation: Since Yudhishthira had been born first, Dhritarashtra anxiously asked whether his own son could ever inherit the throne.

questions.q: According to the epic's account of incarnations, the Kaurava brothers were born as portions of which beings?
options: Rakshasas of Pulastya's line
Vasus cast down by Vasishtha's curse
Gandharvas banished from Indra's court
Nagas exiled from the underworld realms
questions.explanation: The Adi Parva counts the hundred Kauravas as rakshasas of Pulastya's lineage born into human form.

questions.q: When Duryodhana cried out like an ass at birth, which creatures answered him, deepening the dread?
options: Crows and serpents from the palace walls
Asses, vultures, and jackals
Wolves and owls of the night
Elephants and war horses in the stables
questions.explanation: Asses brayed back while vultures and jackals shrieked, and harsh winds and fires added to the omens.

questions.q: What exactly did Gandhari ask of Vyasa when he offered her a boon?
options: A single son mightier than any god
The restoration of her husband's sight
A daughter as wise as the great sages
A hundred sons, each the equal of her husband
questions.explanation: Gandhari asked for one hundred sons each equal to her lord Dhritarashtra, and Vyasa granted it.

questions.q: What size were the hundred and one pieces into which the ball of flesh divided?
options: Each the size of a grain of rice
Each the size of a thumb joint
Each the size of a closed fist
Each the size of a mango stone
questions.explanation: The epic says the sprinkled ball split into a hundred and one pieces, each no larger than a thumb.
